questions.question: Explain how inertia is related to the mass of an object, and provide examples from real-life situations where this concept is applied.
questions.answer: Inertia is the property of an object to resist changes in its motion or state. It is directly proportional to the mass of the object. For instance, a heavy ball will be more difficult to stop than a light one because it has greater inertia due to its larger mass. Similarly, when you are trying to accelerate your car from a standstill, it takes longer to reach high speeds compared to a smaller car with less mass.
answer_outline: Inertia is the resistance of an object to changes in motion or state
It is directly proportional to the mass of the object
Examples: heavy ball vs. light one, accelerating a car

questions.question: Describe the concept of force and its various types, including contact forces and non-contact forces. Provide examples for each type.
questions.answer: Force is a push or pull that causes an object to change its motion or state. It can be classified into two main categories: contact forces and non-contact forces. Contact forces are those that require physical contact between objects to act, such as friction and normal force. Non-contact forces, on the other hand, do not require physical contact, including gravity and magnetism.
answer_outline: Types of forces: contact and non-contact

questions.question: Explain the concept of friction and its different types, including static and kinetic friction. Provide examples for each type.
questions.answer: Friction is a contact force that opposes motion between two surfaces in contact. It can be classified into two main categories: static friction and kinetic friction. Static friction acts on an object when it is stationary, while kinetic friction acts on an object when it is moving.
answer_outline: Types of friction: static and kinetic

questions.question: Describe the concept of work and its relationship with force and displacement. Provide examples to illustrate this relationship.
questions.answer: Work is a measure of the amount of energy transferred from one object to another through a force applied over a certain distance. It is directly proportional to the product of the force applied and the displacement in the direction of the force. For instance, when you push a box across the floor, the work done is equal to the force applied multiplied by the distance moved.

questions.question: Explain the concept of potential energy and its different types, including gravitational potential energy and elastic potential energy. Provide examples for each type.
questions.answer: Potential energy is a form of stored energy that an object possesses due to its position or configuration. It can be classified into two main categories: gravitational potential energy and elastic potential energy. Gravitational potential energy is the energy an object has due to its height, while elastic potential energy is the energy stored in stretched or compressed materials.
answer_outline: Types of potential energy: gravitational and elastic

questions.question: Describe the concept of kinetic energy and its relationship with motion. Provide examples to illustrate this relationship.
questions.answer: Kinetic energy is a form of energy that an object possesses due to its motion. It is directly proportional to the square of the velocity of the object. For instance, when you are riding a bicycle, the kinetic energy of the bike and rider increases as their speed increases.

questions.question: Explain the concept of momentum and its relationship with force and time. Provide examples to illustrate this relationship.
questions.answer: Momentum is a measure of an object's tendency to continue moving in a straight line. It is directly proportional to the product of the mass and velocity of the object. For instance, when you are pushing a cart across the floor, the momentum of the cart increases as its velocity and mass increase.

questions.question: Describe the concept of impulse and its relationship with force and time. Provide examples to illustrate this relationship.
questions.answer: Impulse is a measure of the change in momentum of an object over a certain period of time. It is directly proportional to the product of the force applied and the duration of the application. For instance, when you are hitting a baseball with a bat, the impulse of the ball increases as the force applied and the duration of the hit increase.

questions.question: Explain the concept of energy and its different types, including kinetic energy and potential energy. Provide examples for each type.
questions.answer: Energy is a measure of an object's ability to do work. It can be classified into two main categories: kinetic energy and potential energy. Kinetic energy is the energy an object has due to its motion, while potential energy is the energy an object has due to its position or configuration.
answer_outline: Types of energy: kinetic and potential

questions.question: Describe the concept of power and its relationship with work and time. Provide examples to illustrate this relationship.
questions.answer: Power is a measure of the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred. It is directly proportional to the product of the force applied and the velocity of the object, as well as inversely proportional to the time taken to do the work. For instance, when you are using a drill to drive screws into wood, the power of the drill increases as the force applied and the velocity of the drill increase.
